data.title: Invesco SteelPath MLP Select 40 Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 MLPs total return outperformed the broader equity market

For the quarter, master limited partnerships (MLPs), as measured by the Alerion MLP Index (AMZ), rose 1.81% on a price basis and returned 3.79% when including distributions. The S&P 500 Index rose 2.35% on a price basis and had a total return of 2.66%.

2 Better-than-expected operating results

During the quarter, 62% of midstream sector participants reported third quarter results in line with or better than consensus. Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation & amortization (EBITDA) were about 5% higher than the second quarter and about 7% above the third quarter of 2024.

3 We remain focused on the long-term investment horizon

We believe valuations remain attractive and fundamentals support expectations for cash flow growth. Further, the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) models has been increasing power demand from data centers, which may meaningfully increase demand for natural gas.

Investment objective

The fund seeks total return.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M) 2,011.83

Portfolio managers

Brian Watson, Stuart Cartner

Manager perspective and outlook

  • West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil priced at the Cushing hub ended the quarter at $57.42 per barrel, down 8% from the end of the third quarter and 20% lower than one year ago. The price spread between Brent crude, a proxy for international crude prices, and WTI ended at $3.43 per barrel, narrowing during the quarter. Crude oil priced in Midland, Texas maintained a premium relative to WTI as crude pipeline capacity out of the Permian basin remained sufficient and there appeared to be an incentive for incremental volumes to move toward the Gulf Coast and export markets.
  • Henry Hub natural gas prices ended the quarter at $3.69 per million British thermal units (MMBtu), up 12% from the end of the third quarter and 1% higher than one year ago. Gas pricing in the Permian Basin ended the quarter at negative levels as production growth combined with pipeline maintenance activities reduced available transport from the basin.
  • Natural gas liquids (NGLs) priced at Mont Belvieu ended the quarter at $24.76 per barrel, down 10% from last quarter and 25% lower than one year ago. Prices for all NGL purity products declined during the quarter; propane declined the least while isobutane had the largest price decline for the quarter. Frac spreads, a measure of natural gas processing economics, settled at $0.24 per gallon, down 37% from the end of the third quarter and 51% lower than one year ago.

As of 12/31/25. Holdings are subject to change and are not buy/sell recommendations.

Portfolio positioning

For the quarter, MLPs were up 1.81% on a price basis and returned 3.79% when including distributions. The S&P 500 Index was up 2.35% on a price basis and had a total return of 2.66%.

No US midstream equity (common or preferred) was issued during the quarter via marketed transactions. We estimate a de minimis amount of equity, if any, was issued through “at-the-market” programs. Many sector participants have continued to buy back stock as attractive valuations have in our view persisted. Buyback disclosures historically and typically accompany earnings reports which trail the quarter end by several weeks (for the third quarter of 2025, approximately $1.7 billion of buybacks were reported).

We estimate MLP-focused investment vehicles, including closed-end funds, open-end funds and index-linked products, had approximately $0.2 billion of net inflows during the quarter.

MLP capital investment included an estimated $7-8 billion of organic capital spending. Producer growth plans have remained moderate and have kept traditional midstream growth capital spending requirements measured. However, projects serving rising power demand have been adding incremental growth avenues while leaving sector participants with free cash available for debt retirement, unit repurchases and distribution increases in current and future periods.

During the quarter, the 10-year US Treasury Bond fell by 0.02% to end the period at 4.18%. The MLP yield spread at quarter end, relative to the 10-year Treasury, narrowed by 0.10% to 3.52%. The long-term average (2000-4Q2025) is 4.33%. At quarter end, the AMZ's yield was 7.69%.

We believe the midstream sector offers investors an attractive distribution yield and an improving outlook for cash flow and distribution growth. In our view, these fundamentals are supported by significant and predictable natural gas volume growth through the end of the decade, alongside stable to modest growth in crude oil volumes. Further, midstream sector leverage and distribution coverage metrics have in our view remained healthy, which we believe insulates sector growth plans from market volatility.

Performance highlights

The propane and diversified sub-sectors provided the best relative returns for the quarter, each benefiting from idiosyncratic factors affecting certain sub-sector members. The other and compression sub-sectors had the lowest average returns. Performance in the other group was weighed down by two companies focused on wholesale refined products; the compression sub-sector was likely affected by merger and acquisition activity.

Contributors to performance

NGL Energy Partners LP (NGL)

NGL outperformed after reporting better-than-expected financial and operating results, increasing its cash flow guidance for 2026 and issuing 2027 guidance that contemplates at least 6.9% growth over 2026. NGL has remained focused on further reducing both leverage and its outstanding preferred stock. NGL operates the largest integrated network of large diameter wastewater pipelines, disposal wells and produced water handling systems in the Delaware Basin.

MPLX LP (MPLX)

MPLX outperformed after reporting results that were in line with expectations and announcing a 12.5% increase in its quarterly cash distribution. MPLX has continued to advance its strategy of creating integrated crude oil and natural gas logistics systems.

Targa Resources Corp (TRGP)

TRGP outperformed after reporting better-than-expected financial and operating results and reaffirming company expectations for low double-digit volume growth in 2026. During the quarter, TRGP announced two

strategically complementary acquisitions in the Permian Basin that are expected to boost earnings. TRGP provides natural gas and natural gas liquid services via an integrated asset base with a premium footprint in the Permian basin.

Detractors from performance

Cheniere Energy Inc. (LNG)

LNG underperformed as price and volume outlooks for global natural gas appear to be moderating as new liquefaction facilities are expected to add supply at a faster pace than expected demand growth. LNG's contract portfolio historically largely insulates it from moderating global natural gas prices. LNG owns and operates liquefied natural gas (LNG) terminals and pipelines in Louisiana and Texas.

Genesis Energy LP (GEL)

GEL underperformed after reporting results that fell below expectations following the delayed startup of two offshore production platforms. Both platforms were online in the fourth quarter and production was above expectations. GEL is a diversified midstream energy service provider focused on offshore pipeline transportation & marine transportation.

The Williams Companies Inc (WMB)

WMB underperformed despite reporting financial and operating results generally in line with expectations. WMB advanced several growth project initiatives with its burgeoning power solutions business, as well as new pipeline projects within its core natural gas gathering and transmission segments.

About Risk

Derivatives may be more volatile and less liquid than traditional investments and are subject to market, interest rate, credit, leverage, counterparty, and management risks. An investment in a derivative could lose more than the cash amount invested.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

Although the characteristics of MLPs closely resemble a traditional limited partnership, a major difference is that MLPs may trade on a public exchange or in the over-the-counter market. Although this provides a certain amount of liquidity, MLP interests may be less liquid and subject to more abrupt or erratic price movements than conventional publicly traded securities. The risks of investing in an MLP are similar to those of investing in a partnership and include more flexible governance structures, which could result in less protection for investors than investments in a corporation. MLPs are generally considered interest-rate sensitive investments. During periods of interest rate volatility, these investments may not provide attractive returns.

The Fund is subject to certain other risks. Please see the current prospectus for more information regarding the risks associated with an investment in the Fund.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund's portfolio management, are based on current market conditions and are subject to change without notice. These opinions may differ from those of other Invesco investment professionals.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Sharpe Ratio is a risk-adjusted measure calculated using standard deviation and excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. A higher Sharpe ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. Information Ratio is a measurement of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Standard deviation measures a fund's range of total returns and identifies the spread of a fund's short-term fluctuations. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio's excess return over the benchmark index return. The up and down capture measures how well a manager was able to replicate or improve on periods of positive benchmark returns and how severely the manager was affected by periods of negative benchmark returns. Maximum Drawdown is the maximum observed loss from a high to a low of a portfolio, before a new high is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period.

data.title: Micron: Don't Fall For The Low P/E - It's A Value Trap
data.description:
Introduction

Micron Technology (MU) is an interesting company. At the time of writing, it’s the only company in the S&P 500 with a “straight A” report card on Seeking Alpha’s Quant Factor ratings. It’s got a 4.99/5 Quant rating (ranked #2 out of all stocks covered by the system), an overall “Buy” rating from Seeking Alpha analysts, and a “Strong Buy” average rating from Wall Street analysts (28 rate it a strong buy, 10 buy, 5 hold, and no Wall Street analysts rate it a sell). What’s that adage about when something sounds too good to be true?

For a stock that’s surged nearly 300% in the last year, this has me asking, “What's the catch?” How can the stock appreciate this much and still look so cheap? The answer, in my view, is that Micron is the ultimate siren song stock. I see commentators and analysts mentioning metrics like P/E, EV/EBITDA, and PEG ratios, but the problem is that with this stock in particular, these are not buy signals but warning signs.

In this article, I lay out the rationale for Micron being a tempting but ultimately unwise investment. I then use a valuation model based on a projection of 2030 book value and apply a 1.5x price-to-book multiple on that value and then discount it to the present. Under optimistic assumptions, I get a fair value per share of $264. I don’t include a repricing catalyst or suggest trading the stock right now. But I argue that Micron’s behavior right now is right in line with its historical boom-bust pattern.

Background (Earnings Call)

Micron recently reported what is possibly the best quarter in the history of the semiconductor industry, and paradoxically the stock has since declined significantly. Revenues tripled, and gross margins hit 74.4%, and the company guided for 81% margins. On this news the stock entered a bear market within a matter of days, and as a result is currently one of the ‘cheapest’ stocks in the S&P 500 in terms of forward P/E. So how do we make sense of this apparent discrepancy?

Game Theory of an Oligopoly

There are three major manufacturers of DRAM and High Bandwidth Memory: Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix. Let’s say two of these companies decide to hold back production to keep memory prices high. This leaves the third with a massive incentive to expand production to capture market share. Because one player is likely to break rank in a three-player game, all three players are likely to break rank rather than collude.

Indulge me with an analogy to explain this dynamic. Let’s imagine that only three kids are allowed to sell lemonade in a wealthy neighborhood. In normal times they sell glasses of lemonade for $1 each. But all of a sudden, a massive heat wave hits exactly when construction crews come into the neighborhood to build a few new mansions. The thirsty, exhausted construction workers are willing to pay $5 per glass for the lemonade. The kids can’t make lemonade fast enough. To fill the demand, they need to stop squeezing lemonade with their bare hands and instead buy industrial juicers for $1000 a piece.

What happens in this dynamic? If only one of the kids (let’s call him Timmy) buys the juicer, he makes a killing, and the other kids lose out massively. Because no one wants to lose out, all three kids buy the industrial juicers. The heat wave and construction projects are expected to last a few more weeks, so the kids know there’s likely strong demand in the immediate future. But what happens in the long run? Eventually, all three kids have far too much capacity. To sell their gallons of lemonade before they spoil, the kids engage in a brutal race-to-the-bottom price war, undercutting each other until the cost of a glass of lemonade has crashed to a few cents a piece.

Now imagine a wealthy investor, Mr. Monty P. Moneybags, pulling up in his stretched limo on the hottest day of the year. He sees the line of construction workers circling the block to get a glass of lemonade from Timmy’s Lemonade Stand. Mr. Moneybags does some very flawed back-of-the-handkerchief math: he figures Timmy sells 1,000 glasses of lemonade per day for $5 each, with an 80% margin. That’s $4k per day, or about $1.5 million per year. Mr. Moneybags figures if he offers Timmy $1.5 million to buy the lemonade business, he’ll make his money back within a year. “I’m getting this great business for a 1.0x P/E multiple; what a steal!” Mr. Moneybags has fallen for the cyclical trap: he’s valued a cyclical commodity as though it’s a secular growth story.

This is what the market is worried about with Micron. If you’re an investor thinking about investing in Timmy’s Lemonade Stand, you would be a fool to judge the business based on a snapshot in the middle of a heat/construction wave. You can’t assume that the 1,000 glasses of lemonade sold every day for $5 each is sustainable in the long term. So right now, things look very strong. But the memory market is brutally cyclical. If, as has happened in the past, demand dries up, then the additional capacity built during the boom cycle guarantees a total erosion of margins during the bust cycle. Ironically, Micron’s forward P/E ratio is usually lowest when its stock price is highest. In this case, a low P/E is not a sign of a cheap stock; it’s a sign of a cyclical peak.

Why Is HBM So Expensive Today?

High Bandwidth Memory builds a skyscraper out of traditional DRAM. It’s essentially a new product: SK Hynix was the first to figure out the packaging process, while for quite a while Samsung failed. Micron is actually the smallest player out of the three. The process used to drill microscopic holes in DRAM that is required to turn DRAM into HBM is very difficult and energy intensive. Right now production yields are quite low, which leads to a lot of trashed product. That’s one reason HBM is so expensive at the moment: a low yield rate means there is a significant shortage.

The current argument for Micron’s stock is that HBM is a new type of product with more sustainable pricing power. Admittedly there are some important differences from past cycles—Micron strikes deals with chipmakers for long periods of guaranteed sales. So, at least for 2026, sales are locked-in in advance. In the lemonade analogy, Timmy has pre-sold the lemonade well in advance, so he doesn’t have to worry about collapsing demand in the short term. The construction workers are already on the hook for near-term purchases.

But ultimately it’s still made out of DRAM, and, in my view, the same economic dynamic likely applies to HBM as applies to DRAM. Ironically, increasing yields may actually hurt the market. Right now, chipmakers can’t get enough memory; Nvidia basically has to pay whatever prices Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix ask. But if yield rates increase, then supply immediately increases as well, without having to wait for new capacity to hit the market. Then customers’ pricing power increases overnight.

The irony is that in an oligopoly, what is good at the firm level tends to be self-defeating at the industry level. All three players are individually incentivized to push production yields higher, but if they all do, then they all lose pricing power. An oligopoly makes it difficult to keep yields low artificially since one player would disproportionately gain over the other two by increasing their own yields. If Micron alone could increase its yields while the other two were not able to increase yields, Micron would win temporarily. But I believe the most likely scenario is that each competitor pushes the others to greater efficiency. When that happens, supply increases and the memory market shifts from a seller’s market to a buyer’s market.

Moat Arbitrage

I would argue that in a scenario in which Micron wins, Nvidia (NVDA) wins more, and in a situation in which Nvidia loses, Micron loses more. Let’s examine what happens in each outcome:

AI Demand is Long-Lasting

Let’s say AI exceeds our wildest expectations and data center growth is absolutely insatiable for years to come. The problem for Micron is that it creates huge incentives for HBM capacity to expand to meet demand. When that happens, the massive margins that Micron currently enjoys will still likely vanish eventually. Nvidia, on the other hand, has a totally unique product backed up by a proprietary ecosystem that developers are locked into. Nvidia, therefore, has more control over how much it supplies to the market. It can optimize its CapEx to protect its margins—it doesn't have to surge capacity to a self-defeating extent because it doesn't have to worry as much about competitors with close substitutes. So in a world of surging demand, you'd still rather own the monopolist (or dominant firm, to be precise) than the oligopolist. Consequently, in the event of an AI-driven boom, NVDA > MU in my view.

AI Bubble Bursts

Now let’s think about the bearish scenario. Let’s say companies realize they bought too many GPUs and they decrease or pause orders. This definitely hurts Nvidia, but the company is still protected by its massive proprietary moat and CUDA ecosystem. Even in an upgrade/replacement cycle, I think Nvidia can retain a significant amount of pricing power. So far each new platform (Rubin, Blackwell, etc.) is a significant upgrade on the one before it. For Micron, the capacity being built today ensures memory oversupply in a cycle downturn. To service the fixed costs of this extra capacity, all three memory makers would be forced to slash prices. HBM is improving, but ultimately Micron is unlikely to be able to have a product that is differentiated much from what Samsung or SK Hynix has. In the bubble-bursting scenario, Micron likely loses all its pricing power very quickly. So in an AI bust, NVDA > MU still. If you're interested to see why I think this scenario may be the more likely of the two, check out my recent article discussing the development of AI.

Both companies right now look cheap on paper with low PE and PEG ratios relative to history. But Micron is far more exposed to the cutthroat nature of cyclical commodity economics. Yes, at the moment Micron looks very cheap, but this is the Mr. Moneybags fallacy.

History Repeating Itself

I don’t want to belabor the dot-com bubble analogy, but for Micron I think it is a relevant case study. In June 1998, Micron was trading around $11 per share. By July 2000 it was up to $97 per share. Sound familiar? By 2003, the stock had crashed to $7.50 per share. If you bought at the peak of frenzy in 2000, you would have had to hold the stock for 24 years to make your money back. In the late 1990s, there was insatiable memory demand as we built out the internet. Servers, routers, and home PCs all needed DRAM. But when the build-out slowed, memory prices crashed, taking MU’s stock price down with them. The chart below shows MU's price history from 1995 through 2023, before this latest upturn. You can see that in prior times when MU's stock gained value exponentially, it didn't tend to last.

In the dot-com bubble, compared to companies like Cisco, Micron’s valuation looked entirely reasonable based on metrics like the P/E multiple or EV/EBITDA multiple. But again, investors were looking at a snapshot in time and not factoring in the fundamental patterns of memory cycles. If you’re using a P/E or EV/EBITDA multiple to value Micron, you’re making the same error investors made in the late 90s. Those valuation multiples inherently assume earnings perpetuity. You’re committing the Mr. Moneybags fallacy. This mistake seems to be predictably repeated every time memory is in high demand. MU has seen peak-to-trough declines of 50% or more on six occasions since the 1990s.

Valuation

So what is Micron worth, and how should we value a company that exhibits such extreme cyclicality? From 2016 to 2018, operating income shot up from $234 million to $15 billion. It then dropped to $3 billion in 2020. Then it shot up to $10 billion, then dropped to -$3.5 billion. Now it’s at $28 billion in the most recent fiscal year reported. And forecasts are, of course, even better. But income and cash flow are almost guaranteed to be all over the map. That makes income- or cash flow-based valuation models difficult to use for this stock.

In my valuation model, I try to determine Micron’s book value in 2030 based on a clean surplus assumption. I use this methodology because it can allow us to model the impact of the current boom cycle on book value. Micron’s book value has been far more stable and predictable over time than its income or cash flows have:

To determine the change in book value over time, I assume 40% ROE in 2026, 50% in 2027, 30% in 2028, and 15% in 2029 and 2030 (note that Micron’s 5Y average ROE is 8%, so I’m assuming ROE remains well above historical averages). This grants the bullish assumption that memory demand will remain very high through about 2028 (when new capacity comes online). That gives an expected book value of $310 per share by 2030. Then I apply a 1.5x price-to-book multiple to get a fair value per share of $465 by the end of 2030. This I view as a generous assumption since it assumes that Micron will be able to extract economic profit in perpetuity (if we assumed zero economic profit after 2030, then fair value per share would just equal book value). So a 1.5x multiple of book value assumes that Micron will continue earning in excess of its cost of equity in perpetuity. In reality, there will likely be periods in which Micron has negative ROE followed by periods of booming demand with extremely high ROE.

Discounting that value back to the present using a required return on equity of 12% (derived from a CAPM with a beta of 1.5, a risk-free rate of 4.5%, and an equity risk premium of 5%) gives a fair value today of $264 per share. So the current share price is at about a 35% premium to my fair value based on this model.

Think about what I'm assuming happens: I'm assuming that Micron becomes one of the largest companies in the world by book value. It will have a greater book value than ExxonMobil and JP Morgan have today and slightly less than Saudi Aramco has today. And then Micron will consistently produce returns in excess of the cost of equity on that massive asset base. This is certainly not a bearish assumption. I'm granting an optimistic version of events over the next few years, and yet fair value per share based on those assumptions is still lower than the market price. That suggests that the stock really is priced for perfection.

Risks to My Thesis

The major risk to my thesis is that demand structurally outpaces supply for many more years than expected. In that case, Micron may be well undervalued right now. If data center spending continues its parabolic growth beyond 2029 or 2030, then the expanded capacity being built now for memory production may still not be sufficient to meet demand. But like I said before, I think that Nvidia is a safer bet if one believes that is the likeliest future. Nvidia likely wins if aggressive data center buildout continues for a long period of time. Micron needs both that and constrained memory capacity. Even if we continue rapidly building data centers, that only works for Micron if capacity doesn’t adapt to meet demand. It already is at the moment—all three players in the HBM market are spending billions to expand capacity.

Micron bulls will likely argue that I'm failing to appreciate the depth of the global memory demand shortage and that my valuation model should reflect that. The problem, though, is that such a shortage guarantees an arms race among the big three players. The incentive to be first to expand capacity enough to meet demand is so strong that it makes a 5-10 year memory shortage very unlikely, even if AI infrastructure spending continues to grow year over year.

Conclusion

I’m well aware that this thesis may look downright foolish in the near term, especially if the overall market recovers from the current dip anytime soon. It would not surprise me if Micron’s share price doubles again in the short term. I’m not attaching any potential repricing catalyst to this analysis either. But I am warning investors that you may be buying into a classic trap here. As long as you don’t spring the trap, you might be in the clear. But I believe that eventually the trap will snap shut and punish investors who hold onto the stock for too long, as it has done before.

As Sir John Templeton said, “The four most dangerous words in investing are: ‘This time it’s different.’” In 1995 people thought memory demand would be permanent because everyone was buying a PC. The stock crashed 80%. In 2000, the story was server demand: the stock crashed 93%. In 2018 the cloud boom was going to make memory a permanent institutional product, no longer tied to consumer PC upgrades. The stock crashed nearly 60% then. Now it’s that HBM is a new paradigm, and so memory demand will now really be permanent. Though it’s hard to say when the peak will occur, I believe a similar cycle is likely to play out again.

data.title: Bonds Won't Save You From The Next Recession
data.description:
I started studying economics and finance in 2019, and one of the first things that I learned was that equities tend to have a negative correlation with bonds. Therefore, if you add bonds to your equity portfolio, you are essentially hedging against the downside risk. In theory, if a recession occurs, equities drop, but part of the losses are going to be offset by bond price appreciation. So, if a 100% equity portfolio drops 40% during a severe recession, a 60/40 (60% equities, 40% bonds) portfolio might decline by just 15-20%.

For years, financial advisors promoted the 60/40 portfolio as a way to benefit from bullish periods while reducing your downside risk, but I believe that this strategy no longer works. Why? Because the correlation between bonds and equity is no longer negative. In my opinion, bonds won’t protect you against the next recession.

Where Does This Bias Come From?

To understand why the 60/40 portfolio no longer works to protect you against the downside risk, I believe it is important to first point out why it got so popular.

People love the idea of remaining invested in the stock market while taking less risk thanks to the bond’s exposure. The 60/40 portfolio was the ideal compromise for people with a low-medium risk tolerance, and the performance achieved across the “lost decade” further fueled their conviction.

From 2001 to 2013, the stock market (red line) went nowhere because the US economy faced two significant recessions. But the 60/40 portfolio (blue line) was up about 30% during the same period. Not only that, in fact the downsides were much less pronounced, something that is very important to help you not panic.

After such a great performance during one of the worst periods ever to be invested, it is not a coincidence that the 60/40 portfolio started to gain weight in the financial community. In 2011, Vanguard launched LifeStrategy 60/40, basically an ETF that replicates the 60/40 approach, and had a pretty decent success. You don’t even have to rebalance each weight on your own.

The 60/40 portfolio performed pretty well also during the Covid crash and protected investors from higher losses. So, if this portfolio worked during the last 3 recessions, why am I saying that it won’t work in the future?

A Hypothesis That Is Far From Obvious

The biggest problem with this strategy is that it takes for granted that the macroeconomic context won’t change. And just to be clear, for macroeconomic context, I am not referring to small changes in the interest rates but a structural shift in the economy that takes decades to get back to the previous levels.

Finance courses teach you that bonds will protect you against a stock market decline, but just because this was the case from 2000 to 2021. The entire financial industry is set on this assumption because it has forgotten how things were in the previous century. Take a look at this chart; I find it enlightening.

As you can see, over the last 100 years, the correlation between stocks and bonds was actually positive most of the time. In other words, a negative correlation is not the norm, or at least it is not as obvious as we might think. At this point a question arises: what’s the most important factor that affects the equity/bond correlation? The inflation rate, and the following chart proves it.

On the Y axis we have the 5-year equity/bond correlation, and on the X axis we have the 5-year annual inflation rate. As you can see, the results are quite evident:

  • During 1970-2000 (green dots), the 5-year annual inflation rate was never below 2%, and it was above 5% most of the time. During that market cycle that lasted 3 decades, the correlation between equity/bonds has always been positive (green dots always above 0). Simply put, bonds wouldn’t have helped you to face the downside risk.
  • During 1929-1970 (blue dots), we have had mixed results. The Great Depression (1929) caused a severe deflation, which was favorable for bonds, but after WW2, the US economy inflated the debt away. That was definitely negative for bonds performance. Overall, even though this period of four decades suffered a major deflation, most of the time the equity-bonds correlation was positive. High inflation was much more frequent.
  • During 2000-2021 (yellow dots), the game changed. Most of the time the equity-bond correlation was negative, so buying bonds was a smart way to diversify your portfolio. During this market phase, the 5-year annual inflation has almost always been below 3%.

In light of these considerations, we can make a fundamental observation: bonds protect your equity portfolio as long as the inflation rate stays below 3%.

Over the years investors took for granted that the inflation rate would have always fluctuated between 1% and 3%, but history is actually telling us that this is not the norm. Let me show you the previous chart once again.

As you can see, the distribution of the yellow dots (2000-2021) is much more concentrated compared to 1926-1970 and 1970-2000. So, if we really want to draw some conclusions here, I would argue that the anomaly is what we have experienced over the last two decades.

Understanding in which long-term macroeconomic cycle we are makes all the difference, and I believe that in 2022 we entered a new phase similar to 1970-2000 (2022 was the worst year for a 60/40 portfolio since 1937).

As you can see, TLT (an ETF composed of US long-term bonds) didn’t offset the 2022 stock market decline, and that was kind of surprising. Weren’t bonds supposed to protect us when hard times come? At first, I believed that 2022 was an anomaly, but as time went by, my opinion changed. We had other stock market declines over the following 4 years, and TLT has been helpful just during Liberation Day. Now, the stock market is in correction territory, and TLT is once again following the same direction.

In other words, a structural change in the macroeconomic context doesn’t happen overnight but over the years. The year 2022 was the turning point, but over the last years we had several indications that support my thesis. Here is another dot graph that helps you understand the evolution of this new phase.

As you can see, the period 2000-2021 (blue dots) showed a negative correlation between bonds and equities most of the time, and we already knew that. But the period October 2021-February 2023 (pink dots) presented a major shift: dots were heading upward. With the March 2023-January 2025 (purple dots) period, I believe we had the confirmation: dots were stably in the upper area of the chart, where the correlation is significantly positive. This is the gradual shift I was talking about.

Final Thoughts

People believe that diversification is about how many assets you own, but the most important factor is how correlated they are to each other. If you buy 100 assets that are positively correlated, you are not really diversifying. And in this phase, you are not really diversifying your equity portfolio if you buy TLT.

At this point the $1 million question is, when will TLT become a valid hedge against recessions once again? This is not something that I can predict with certainty, but I am really confident that it won’t happen anytime soon. As I previously showed you, this kind of cycle lasts decades, and we have just entered the fourth year.

In addition, the Middle East crisis is intensifying, and there is nothing that makes me think that inflation won’t go significantly up over the next months. As I have stated from day 1, this war shouldn’t be underestimated, and it is consistent with the new macroeconomic context in which bonds can’t protect you against falling equities.

We are likely going through a period where inflation will systematically be higher than 2%, and we might even face negative real yields at some point. In fact, inflating the debt away is one of the few options governments have to make their huge debt more sustainable. (This is something that I discussed in depth in a recent article).

During inflationary periods, gold tends to be a better hedge compared to bonds, especially if central banks can’t increase interest rates at the same pace as inflation. I believe this is the case, as most developed economies present a huge debt compared to GDP, and dealing with higher interest payments would be the final blow.

data.title: SPX Call Demand Jumps On Optimism
data.description:
By Mandy Xu

Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities gained across asset classes last week as the Iran war dragged into its fourth week. Oil volatility increased, with WTI 1M implied vol up 10 pts to 81% on the back of continued demand for upside calls as traders positioned for potential prolonged disruption to oil supply. Gold volatility jumped higher, with GLD 1M implied vol increasing over 9 pts to near a 5-year high of 41%. As we highlighted last week, positioning in gold options has shifted notably, with demand for puts picking up in recent weeks amidst the geopolitical turmoil. GLD 1M skew (25-delta ratio) has steepened to almost a 10-year high after being persistently inverted for most of the past two years. Rates volatility gained modestly as traders weighed the higher inflation backdrop with the hit to the growth outlook, while credit volatility continued its upward climb. VIXHY Index has more than tripled since the beginning of the year to a high of 320 bps vol – higher than the Q4 2018 growth scare (when SPX Index sold off 20%).

Equity Volatility: The VIX Index jumped 4.3 pts to 31% last week, ending the week at its highest level since last year’s April sell-off. Notably, however, there’s been very little panic in the options market. Even when SPX Index fell 3.4% on Thu/Fri, the bid to volatility came more from the upside than the downside as investors positioned for a potential rebound (aka “the TACO Trade”). In fact, put skew and put convexity both declined as investors used the pullback to monetize existing hedges. SPX 1M put skew (25-delta vs. 50-delta ratio) has now fallen to near a 1-year low (2nd percentile over the past year). Instead, we saw demand for upside calls pick up going into the weekend, adding almost 2 pts to the 5.7 pt increase in the VIX Index on Thu/Fri. See Exhibit 2. Will the “TACO Trade” work this time? The optimism in the equity options market contrasts with the caution we’re seeing in the cross-asset vol market, with the oil market signaling potentially an extended period of supply disruption and the credit market starting to price in a more severe growth slowdown. Within equities, EM countries are bearing the brunt of the energy shock, with EEM 1M implied volatility nearing its “Liberation Day” high of 42% already (vs. the VIX Index which is still 20+ pts below its Apr’25 peak). The EEM-SPX 1M implied volatility spread widened to a 10-year high of 16% last week (vs. avg spread of 3.7%).

Correlation: Stocks are becoming more correlated as macro headlines overshadow fundamentals, with the COR1M Index gaining another 3 pts last week to 42%. More concerning is the decline in equity-rates correlation, which has flipped to negative as inflation worries have hit both stocks and bonds simultaneously. The 1-month rolling correlation between SPX Index and the 10Y Tsy yield has dropped from +72% to -53% (see Exhibit 3).

Cross-Asset Volatility Monitor

Cross-Asset Volatility Snapshot (10Y Lookback)

How to read this chart:

The chart shows how various asset class implied volatilities are trading relative to their own history, using a 10-year lookback.

The volatilities are ranked by z-scores, which compares the current volatility level for an asset versus its own 10-year history. A z-score of +2, for example, would mean that the current volatility level is trading 2 standard deviations above its long-term average (i.e. rich) while a z-score of -2 would mean it’s trading 2 standard deviations below average (i.e. cheap)

Because each asset class volatility trades at different levels and often defined differently (e.g. lognormal vs. normal vol, price vs. bps vol), by standardizing using zscores, we’re able to compare different asset class volatilities on the same scale - and see at a glance if there are any divergences or dislocations in the cross-asset volatility markets

data.title: CCD: Convertibles Can Leverage Equity Exposure, Time To Buy The Dip
data.description:
The Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income Fund (CCD) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with income through exposure to convertible and corporate bonds, diversified across multiple sectors through strategic asset allocation. The strategy has the ability to provide investors with equity-like exposure as part of their fixed income allocation, potentially geared towards risk-taking investors seeking differentiated returns. With global supply chains being disrupted by the Iranian war, the equity markets have faced significant pressure, resulting in negative pricing action for CCD. Despite the near-term headwinds, I believe investors may opportunistically build a position in CCD with the expectation of a market turnaround as events in the Middle East are resolved; I am recommending CCD with a Buy rating. Before purchasing shares, investors should consider the risks involved in holding CCD, as the strategy’s performance may be significantly influenced by further equity price decline.

Investment Thesis for CCD

Fundamentally, convertibles will inherently trade similarly to the equity markets given the underlying structure of the bonds. Convertible bonds are generally linked to the value of the underlying shares based on the conversion rate, or the rate at which the bond will convert into shares at the time of maturity. As a result of this, these bond issuances may generally trade similarly to the equity markets, presenting investors with differentiated performance with respect to investment-grade and plain vanilla issuances.

While this can be an appealing feature during periods of positive pricing action in the equity markets, the bonds may face similar directional change when the equity markets sell off, as exhibited in the total return chart below. Comparing 6-month performance, CCD has far outpaced the S&P 500 (SPX), though it has recently experienced a similar sell-off in recent weeks, likely the result of the global supply chain disruption with the Iranian war taking shape.

This could become a challenging market environment for CCD going forward if the war remains ongoing and intensifies despite signs of talks between the US and Iran.

I believe the greatest pressure on the domestic market is heightened oil prices, which may add significant uncertainty to the forward rate of inflation that may hinder economic development for 2026. The rationale is that higher oil prices may pressure consumers in relation to their discretionary spending budgets, potentially imposing greater risk to the consumer-focused market. In addition to this, higher oil prices may impact the industrial sector, specifically transportation, petrochemicals, and other markets that rely on cheap oil as an input.

In addition to heightened oil prices, other commodities may impact the global market and, in turn, impact the domestic market environment. While domestic natural gas prices have been tame since the start of the war, international benchmarks have experienced substantial increases in gas prices, which may disrupt global productivity with higher electricity costs.

Another resource that may face supply constraints due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is helium, a critical gas used in photolithography used for the printing of semiconductor circuits. Some of the top holdings within CCD include convertible bonds issued by Western Digital (WDC), Lumentum (LITE), MSK Inc. (MKSI), and Seagate (STX), which may be exposed to the helium shortage.

Overall, companies’ issuances held in CCD may be exposed to the global supply chain risks resulting from the Iranian war, potentially negatively impacting performance in the near term. While I don’t expect this war to be ongoing, there’s no telling just how long the Strait of Hormuz will be shut to the global economy. With rippling impacts on international trade and the supply of resources, I suspect inflationary pressures in the US may rise over the coming months, potentially negatively impacting the equity markets as well as the convertibles held in CCD.

That being said, timing the end of the war isn’t my area of expertise, and as an investor, we should be opportunistic when others are fearful. The fact of the matter is that CCD has declined by nearly -9.5% from its recent high in February 2026, potentially presenting an opportunity for investors seeking to build a position at a relatively lower price point. While CCD could continue to trend lower, particularly when considering the technical signals presented in the Ripster Cloud, averaging into a position over time may be an ideal opportunity as the fund declines.

Structurally, I don’t believe the impact from the war will negatively affect the quality of the bond issuances. On the other hand, because the convertible bonds are priced with respect to the share conversion rate, continued pressure in the equity markets will, in turn, affect the pricing of the convertible bond issuances.

The bottom line is that I believe investors may consider adding to their positions in CCD as the price faces pressure in anticipation of the market turning around. This will likely be an event-driven strategy, primarily associated with the war in Iran and the supply chain disruption tied to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Any signs of an end to the war or alleviation in the supply chain may positively influence the equity markets and CCD.

About Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income Fund

CCD was launched by Calamos on March 27, 2015, on the NASDAQ Exchange. The strategy has a total expense ratio of 237bps, inclusive of a 100bps management fee and 131bps leverage fee. CCD currently has $906mm in managed assets ($621mm in share assets) when applying the 31.43% leverage rate with an average daily trading volume of 84k shares.

The strategy pays out a robust monthly distribution annualized at $2.34/share over the last twelve months, yielding 11.35%.

Risks Related to CCD

CCD is a managed closed-end fund designed to invest across convertible and high-yield corporate bonds, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Issuances held within the strategy may provide a similar risk profile and trade with respect to the equity markets, potentially exposing investors to greater price volatility relative to investment-grade corporate bonds. CCD is actively managed, exposing investors to manager risk and their ability to appropriately select and weight securities. Global supply chain risks may result in continued challenges to the equity markets and negatively influence the price performance of CCD. Convertible issuances may be particularly sensitive to the share price of the underlying securities as a result of their respective conversion rates, potentially impacting the value of the bonds if the equity market were to significantly decline.

Final Thoughts

Investing in CCD can be an opportunistic investment strategy following the significant price decline in recent weeks. With the equity markets facing greater volatility resulting from heightened oil prices following the military mobilization in Iran, shares may face continued pressure until events in the region are resolved. Following the price decline in CCD, I believe investors may opportunistically buy CCD or average into a position with the expectation of a market turnaround in the future; I am recommending CCD with a Buy rating.

data.title: Tesla May Beat Deliveries, But This Could Hurt The Bottom Line
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) will soon release its Q1 2026 production and deliveries, and I expect the company to beat analyst estimates due to one key reason that I discuss in this article. However, for the same reason, I expect the company's profit margins to miss analyst estimates in Q1 2026 and remain depressed in the coming periods.

Previous Coverage

I most recently covered the company in my article Tesla: Challenging 2026 Ahead, in which I discussed Tesla's intensifying global competition, weakening demand, and persistent delays in autonomy and robotics, and assigned a Strong Sell rating. Tesla has declined by 20 percent since my article.

Q1 2026 Forecast

I expect Tesla to deliver more than 370,000 vehicles in Q1 2026, somewhat above the analyst consensus of 365,645. The following table shared by Tesla shows the average, median, and standard deviation of 23 analyst estimates:

The standard deviation of 25,941 indicates that analyst estimates have a wide range. In a normal distribution, approximately 68% of the data falls within one standard deviation, meaning 16 estimates likely were between 337,000 and 389,000, and seven estimates were outside of that range. This is a surprisingly wide range of estimates for a mega-cap company followed around the world by dozens, if not hundreds, of professional analysts as well as countless retail investors. This is important because, if the current quarter's estimates show such high uncertainty, the estimates for future periods may not be reliable.

I believe that the following topic helped Tesla's deliveries but will materially and adversely impact Tesla's profitability in Q1 2026 (figures expected April 22nd) and future periods.

0% APR and Subprime Financing

Ever since interest rates surged in 2022, Tesla has had to increasingly rely on price cuts and subvented (subsidized) interest rate financing to keep demand from falling sharply, but this comes at a cost to Tesla's profit margins and its bottom line.

In January 2023, Tesla slashed worldwide prices for its most popular vehicles by up to 20 percent, and Tesla continued to lower prices in all major markets throughout 2023 and 2024 in a series of price cuts. In 2025, Tesla expanded subvented interest rates and even interest-free financing offers in China, the U.S., and Europe. In October 2025, Tesla rolled out cheaper "Standard" Model 3 and Model Y trims, about $5,000 below the previous base models, following the expiration of the generous EV tax credits in the United States.

Tesla Is Financing Subprime Credit

I have not seen this topic widely discussed, but I believe it is one of the costliest steps that Tesla had to take in recent years to shore up demand.

Historically, Tesla limited subvented interest-rate financing offers to "well-qualified buyers with excellent credit" profiles, but in Q1, I noticed that Tesla began offering a 2.99 percent financing rate to buyers with credit scores less than 640, or "Fair" credit profiles, for loan terms of up to 72 months.

If the buyer selects a Term greater than 72 months, namely 84 months, then the rate jumps to 11.67 percent, which I believe is the market rate for buyers with Fair credit profiles, as the following screenshot shows:

Tesla appears to be moving further down the credit spectrum by subsidizing the interest rate for buyers with Fair credit, lowering the monthly payments, in order to keep its deliveries from declining at a faster rate. This dynamic likely requires Tesla to make an upfront payment, the present value of the difference in monthly payments over the term of the loan, to third-party financiers to be able to offer subsidized rates to potential buyers.

This practice, however, comes at a cost. Given the difference between the subvented and the market interest rates, I estimate that the one-time payment from Tesla to the third-party financier could be as high as $10,000 for buyers with Fair credit profiles, financing a $45,000 loan for 72 months. This amount declines as the size of the down-payment increases.

I believe that Tesla books this expense to Cost of Sales with each delivery, so I expect this practice to pressure Tesla Automotive's profit margins in 2026. Depending on the prevalence of buyers with Fair credit in Tesla's buyer pool, which only Tesla knows, this dynamic could compress Tesla's automotive gross margins and company-wide operating margin by multiple percentage points.

As a sensitivity analysis, if buyers with Fair credit account for 5 percent, 10 percent, or 15 percent of Tesla's financed deliveries, and the size of Tesla's upfront payment to the third-party financier is in the several thousands of dollars range, then the downward pressure on Tesla's automotive gross margin would be 50-100 basis points in the low-adoption case, 100-200 basis points in the medium-adoption case, and 200-300 basis points in the high-adoption case, respectively.

Risks To My Bear Thesis

First, even though Tesla is offering subvented rates to buyers with Fair credit, it is not advertising the practice, so the prevalence may be low, limiting the downward pressure on Tesla's Automotive gross margin.

Second, if automotive loan interest rates decline overall, then the amount of the one-time payment from Tesla to third-party financiers would decline.

Third, Tesla's profitability in other departments, namely Energy and Services, or a material increase in FSD subscriptions, could partly offset the downward pressure from price cuts and subvented interest-rate loans.

Bottom Line

Tesla is dipping deeper into the credit spectrum to generate demand for its vehicles in the face of intensifying competition, but subsidizing fair-credit buyers is costly and not discussed widely across the investment community.

I expect Tesla to beat analyst consensus for Q1 2026 deliveries, but in the following months, the reality of low profit margins and possible net losses may become front and center. I maintain my $55 per share price target and Strong Sell rating.

data.title: Argan Surges 38% Post-Earnings: 3 Best Stocks With Similar Growth Potential
data.description:
Industry Drivers Support Construction and Engineering Growth

Alpha Picks stock winner, Argan, Inc. (AGX), surged 38% Friday, reminding investors that not all stocks are impacted by the same macro pressures. While markets remain focused on higher oil prices and inflation arising from geopolitical uncertainty, AGX delivered a blowout earnings report highlighted by a $2.9 billion project backlog, which provides clear earnings visibility into demand for large-scale power infrastructure.

This type of sector- and industry-specific strength reinforces an important theme in today’s market. Select segments of the economy, particularly within Industrials, are benefiting from large-scale infrastructure investment trends that are not directly impacted by short-term macro volatility. In industries like Construction and Engineering, multi-year project backlogs can translate into earnings strength and continued price momentum.

In this article, I highlight three Quant Strong Buys in the Construction and Engineering industry that are well-positioned to capitalize on these demand trends and potentially follow AGX’s lead.

How I Selected the Best Industrials Stocks to Buy Now

To select the best stocks to follow AGX’s lead, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and targeted Quant Strong Buys. Then I selected the sector & industry filter so I could sort for targeted stocks in the Industrials sector and the Construction and Engineering industry. I dug further to find compelling backlog stories that reveal strong earnings visibility.

1. Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL)

  • Market Capitalization: $12.89B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 7 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 1 out of 37

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

One of my Alpha Picks holdings, Sterling Infrastructure, is a market leader in large-scale E-infrastructure solutions like data centers, as well as transportation and manufacturing facilities. What sets STRL apart from many of its competitors is its increasing focus on higher-margin growth markets tied to AI, reshoring, and digital infrastructure. Like AGX, Sterling’s strong backlog growth and sector-leading margins signal potential for earnings growth in 2026. This leads us to STRL’s Quant Factor strengths in Profitability and Momentum.

Sterling’s 19.49% Return on Total Capital, which is 179% ahead of sector peers, demonstrates its ability to invest its capital effectively into high-margin projects. Meanwhile, Sterling’s sector-leading Cash From Operations provides the financial flexibility needed to reinvest in new growth opportunities. In their recent quarterly earnings report, Joseph Cutillo, CEO, remarked on how the company achieved "strong revenue growth of over 32% and adjusted diluted EPS growth of over 53%." He also highlighted, "Full year gross margins reached 23%, and adjusted EBITDA margins exceeded 20% for the first time in our history." Cutillo also noted that 2025 was the fifth consecutive year of over 35% adjusted EPS growth. This profitability and growth story helps to support the company’s 'A+' price momentum.

STRL crushes its Industrials sector peers so far in 2026 with its three-month performance of 32.76% compared to -3.49% for the sector median. Price momentum is a Quant Factor because it is predictive of future performance, especially when combined with other factors, such as Growth and Profitability. This is in part attributable to strong investor demand and positive sentiment, which can drive further buying and price appreciation in the short term, especially when earnings support the growth stories like AGX and STRL. When momentum is supported by strong fundamentals and positive industry trends, STRL stands out as a sector leader, which sets the stage for another high-quality operator in the Construction and Engineering industry.

2. MasTec, Inc. (MTZ)

  • Market Capitalization: $24.60B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 25 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 3 out of 37

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

MasTec, Inc. is a leading U.S.-based infrastructure company focused on energy, utility, and communications projects, including renewable energy and oil and gas pipeline construction. Its exposure extends to data center-related work, which opens further growth opportunities. With a growing backlog, MTZ is well-positioned to expand profitability and maintain its earnings visibility into 2026, which is demonstrated by its ‘A-’ Growth Factor grade.

MTZ’s outstanding growth metrics and impressive backlog were highlighted by CEO Jose Mas in their February earnings call. Mas stated:

In summary, we exceeded guidance again in revenue, EBITDA and EPS, highlighting another strong execution quarter and year for MasTec. This strong result is in part a testament to the scale and diversification MasTec has achieved over time, and we are excited about our outlook for 2026 and beyond given the clear long-term positive market conditions across all of the end markets we serve.

The company’s forward EBITDA Growth is 19.27%, which is more than double the sector median, while its forward long-term earnings growth (3-5Y CAGR) of 31% is over 150% higher than sector peers. Mas supported its forward growth, stating, "On a full year basis, backlog was up over $4.5 billion, a 33% annual increase. Sequentially, backlog was up over $2 billion, representing a 1.6x book-to-bill." Continuation of this growth can support the company’s outstanding momentum.

At over 40% appreciation for the past three months, MTZ supports its 'A+' momentum grade, putting in greater than 40% performance over the past three months and about 166% for the past year. Continued expansion in clean energy and infrastructure construction can also create long-term demand in the short term as well as the long run, leading into our third look at Industrials sector leaders.

3. MYR Group Inc. (MYRG)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.25B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 14 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 2 out of 37

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

A construction and engineering industry leader for more than 130 years, MYR Group provides large-scale electrical construction services for utilities and commercial customers, including data centers. This gives MYRG a strong competitive advantage, as backlog strength is supported by rising power demand. The company’s exposure to renewable energy diversifies its long-term growth potential. From a Quant perspective, MYRG’s Growth and Revisions scorecards point to more momentum potential.

MYRG’s forward EBITDA Growth of over 38% is more than four times the sector median’s measure of just above 8%, while the company’s sector-crushing forward Operating Cash Flow of 46.65% stands to help fund its pursuit of new opportunities. At its recent quarterly earnings call, President and CEO Richard Swartz highlighted a "steady backlog of $2.8 billion at the end of 2025," driven by a "healthy bidding environment and the continued investment in infrastructure to meet the growing electrification needs across the U.S. and Canada." Like AGX and our featured Construction and Engineering stocks, this backstory can support a continuation of the company’s stellar price momentum.

MYRG’s 136% one-year price performance looks to extend to another year of sector-leading momentum as the past three months’ gains surpass 21%. The company’s disciplined operations, strong backlog, and positioning within critical infrastructure markets provide potential for further momentum in 2026, bringing us to the broader conclusion on these Quant Strong Buy stocks.

Conclusion: Industrials Sector Maintains Growth Potential

AGX’s 38% surge following Friday’s earnings highlights a broader theme, which is the potential for Industrials, particularly select Construction and Engineering companies, to benefit from cyclical demand. Furthermore, while the geopolitical and macro environment weighs on market sentiment, high-quality companies like STRL, MTZ, and MYRG with strong backlogs can attract investor attention and continue pre-war price momentum. In a market dominated by uncertainty and volatility, these Quant Strong Buys offer a compelling case for maintaining exposure to high-quality industrial growth stocks.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives or consider PRO Quant Portfolio, which we launched in June. data.title: Yesway Restarts IPO For Debt Reduction And Continued Expansion
data.description:
Yesway Is Growing Profitably

Yesway, Inc. (YSWY) has filed to raise public capital from an IPO of its Class A common stock, per an S-1 registration statement recently filed with the SEC.

The company operates a network of convenience stores mostly in lower-tier and rural markets in the central and southern United States.

Revenue is growing moderately, but the firm is profitable and is pursuing both organic growth (new store development) and acquisition growth drivers to expand its footprint.

The IPO will likely be in demand from institutional investors, assuming management’s valuation assumptions are reasonable.

What Does Yesway Do?

Yesway is expanding its network of convenience stores and fueling stations operating via its brands Yesway and Allsup’s.

The firm previously began an IPO process in late 2021, as I wrote about here, but ended that process due to unfavorable IPO market conditions at the time.

Since then, Yesway has grown its network by a net of 16 stores, while total revenue has risen from a run rate of about $1.6 billion in 2021 to $2.7 billion in 2025, and total liabilities have also risen materially.

Operating margin has also increased since the original IPO attempt, as has operating cash flow.

The company had 419 stores in seven states as of December 31, 2025, per the map below:

Yesway sells both third-party convenience store merchandise and its house brand products.

The firm was founded in 2015 by private equity firm Brookwood and has grown substantially through 27 acquisitions while building organically (90 stores by the end of 2025).

However, if Yesway wants to expand its geographic footprint, it would have logistics constraints in that it would have to build (or buy) distribution and other capabilities to support the expanded footprint while building brand density for marketing and logistics efficiencies of scale.

The company's 2025 revenue contribution by segment is shown in the pie chart below:

For convenience store operators with fuel operations, the fuel is typically a low-margin business designed to bring in traffic, whereas the inside merchandise sales are the relatively high-margin part of the business.

In Yesway’s example, the inside merchandise is producing the majority of the gross profit, with the 2025 gross margin at 35.2%.

Compared to competitors Murphy USA and Casey’s, Yesway lands in the middle of the two in terms of gross margin, with Casey’s at around 41% and Murphy at 31%, according to recent reports.

Yesway’s co-founder, Chairman, President, and CEO is Mr. Thomas N. Trkla, who is also Chairman and CEO of Brookwood, which has invested a fair market value investment of $823 million to date.

What is Yesway’s Market?

The company operates in the convenience store and refueling market in North America, which was estimated at approximately $1 trillion in size in 2021.

The global convenience store market was expected to grow at a CAGR of 5.6% from 2022 to 2028.

By region, the Asia Pacific region is expected to grow the fastest, at a CAGR of 6.4% during the forecast period.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth trajectory of the U.S. convenience store market through 2026, by product type:

The market is expected to continue its growth path as consumers seek ready-made meals or snacks while fueling their automobiles.

By segment, the foodservice segment is expected to produce the highest CAGR through 2028, at 6.4%.

The convenience store market is highly fragmented, with greater than 50% of stores operated by single-unit owners, but the list below includes various major players:

  • Casey's General Stores, Inc. (CASY).

  • Alimentation Couche-Tard Inc. (ANCTF).

  • Murphy USA Inc. (MUSA).

  • Parkland Corporation.

  • 7-Eleven.

  • FamilyMart.

  • ARKO Petroleum Corp. (APC).

  • Major fuel companies.

Recent industry trends include the continued consolidation by larger operators, an increased focus on foodservice as a driver of traffic, and only gradual technology adoption.

The company's strengths include its geographic focus, positioning in rural & suburban markets with reduced competition, and differentiated foodservice offerings in Allsup’s.

Weaknesses include its reliance on fuel-oriented traffic, lower-density rural market dynamics, and operational complexity with its multi-format stores.

Opportunities include new store development along with M&A potential, foodservice expansion, and private-label growth and optimization via improved technology and data.

Threats are electric vehicle adoption, regulatory changes, commodity price volatility, and competitors also seeking to acquire existing operators in local regions.

Yesway’s Recent Financial Results

The company's financial results show moderate top-line revenue growth, higher gross margin, increasing net margin, and improved cash flow from operations, as the table shows here:
